key_insight: The paper turns SRO design in high-entropy alloys into a differentiable optimization problem, enabling direct gradient-based inverse design from target structure to target property.
engineering_relevance: This gives materials engineers a faster, scalable route to design multi-principal-element alloys with prescribed stiffness behavior instead of relying on slow random-swap search and separate property screening.
method: Replace reverse Monte Carlo with continuous atom-occupancy variables and gradient descent to match target short-range order.
Add a thermodynamic correction term so the designed SRO remains physically realistic across many-element alloys.
Use a small neural network to map composition + SRO statistics to mechanical properties, closing the loop for property-driven design.
evidence_signal: Across nine FCC and BCC alloys, the pipeline captured SRO-driven stiffness shifts from -20% to +57%, and on a Co-Cr-Ni case it matched three of four target stiffness values within 6%; 4000-atom tests were also 6× faster and 8× more accurate than random-swap search.
watch_item: Accuracy depends on adequate supercell size and model assumptions: the abstract notes 108-atom cells can mislead, while at least 864 atoms were needed to recover the correct stiffness trends.
paper_title: Differentiable inverse design of short-range order in high-entropy alloys: from target sro to target property
